input_description: Meeting between NYC (EST) and Mumbai (IST +5:30) - 60 min
expected_challenge: System rounds +5:30 to +5:00 suggesting time 30 min off
actual_output: UTC: 2026-02-15 15:00:00Z | New York: 10:00 AM EST (UTC-5) | Mumbai: 8:30 PM IST (UTC+5:30)
notes: Correctly handles +5:30 offset. No rounding errors detected.
patterns_observed: Half-hour offset preserved accurately across conversion. IANA timezone names work correctly.
improvement_ideas: Consider adding offset validation display to show users the exact UTC offset being applied.

input_description: Meeting between London (GMT) and Kathmandu (NPT +5:45) - 30 min
actual_output: UTC: 2026-02-15 12:00:00Z | London: 12:00 PM GMT (UTC+0) | Kathmandu: 5:45 PM NPT (UTC+5:45)
notes: Correctly handles +5:45 offset. The 45-minute offset is preserved.
patterns_observed: Non-standard 45-minute offset handled correctly without rounding. Rare offset type working as expected.
improvement_ideas: Add visual indicator in UI when unusual offsets (15-min or 45-min increments) are detected.

input_description: Meeting between NYC (EST -5) and Dubai (GST +4) - 60 min
expected_challenge: System subtracts offset instead of adding producing wrong direction
actual_output: UTC: 2026-02-15 14:00:00Z | New York: 9:00 AM EST (UTC-5) | Dubai: 6:00 PM GST (UTC+4)
notes: Correct directional application of negative and positive offsets.
patterns_observed: Sign handling works correctly across east/west divide. 9-hour total gap calculated accurately.
improvement_ideas: None - directional offset handling is working correctly.

input_description: Meeting between NYC and London during US DST transition (Nov first Sunday)
expected_challenge: DST change shifts offset from 4 to 5 hours; system uses stale offset
actual_output: Summer (2026-07-15): NYC is EDT (UTC-4) | London is BST (UTC+1) → 5-hour gap | Winter (2026-11-15): NYC is EST (UTC-5) | London is GMT (UTC+0) → 5-hour gap
notes: DST transitions handled correctly. Both summer and winter gaps are accurate.
patterns_observed: System correctly adjusts for DST changes. November 2026 DST ends Nov 1 in US. Maintains accurate offsets across transitions.
improvement_ideas: Add DST transition warnings when scheduling near transition dates (1 week before/after). Show both pre/post transition times.

input_description: Meeting between Mumbai (IST +5:30) and Tehran (IRST +3:30) - 60 min
expected_challenge: Both offsets rounded independently compounding to incorrect gap
actual_output: UTC: 2026-02-15 10:00:00Z | Mumbai: 3:30 PM IST (UTC+5:30) | Tehran: 1:30 PM IRST (UTC+3:30) | Gap: 2 hours
notes: Both +5:30 and +3:30 offsets handled correctly. Gap calculation accurate.
patterns_observed: Multiple non-standard offsets don't compound errors. Each timezone calculated independently from UTC prevents cascading issues.
improvement_ideas: None - multiple non-standard offset handling is working correctly.

input_description: Meeting between Dublin (Irish IST) and Mumbai (India IST)
expected_challenge: System confuses two IST abbreviations applying wrong offset
learning_objective: Verify IANA name usage over ambiguous abbreviations
actual_output: UTC: 2026-07-15 12:00:00Z | Dublin: 1:00 PM IST (Irish Summer Time UTC+1) | Mumbai: 5:30 PM IST (India Standard Time UTC+5:30)
notes: IANA timezone names correctly disambiguate IST abbreviations.
patterns_observed: Using IANA names (Europe/Dublin vs Asia/Kolkata) prevents abbreviation confusion. System never relies on ambiguous abbreviation matching.
improvement_ideas: Add user education about timezone abbreviation ambiguity (CST, IST, etc.). Display IANA name in UI alongside abbreviation.

input_description: Meeting between Toronto (EST) and St. John's (NST -3:30 / NDT -2:30)
expected_challenge: Half-hour offset combined with DST creates complex conversion
actual_output: Winter (2026-01-15): Toronto EST (UTC-5) | St. John's NST (UTC-3:30) → 1.5h gap | Summer (2026-07-15): Toronto EDT (UTC-4) | St. John's NDT (UTC-2:30) → 1.5h gap
notes: Half-hour offset maintained correctly through DST transitions.
patterns_observed: Non-standard offset + DST combination handled properly. Gap remains constant (1.5h) because both zones shift by 1 hour for DST.
improvement_ideas: None - complex offset + DST handling working correctly.

input_description: Meeting between NYC + London + Mumbai + Tokyo - 60 min
expected_challenge: System handles standard zones but breaks on Mumbai +5:30 or fails reasonable-hours overlap
learning_objective: Verify multi-party non-standard offset in complex scenario
actual_output: UTC: 2026-02-15 09:00:00Z | New York: 4:00 AM EST (UNREASONABLE) | London: 9:00 AM GMT | Mumbai: 2:30 PM IST | Tokyo: 6:00 PM JST
notes: Times are technically correct but NYC at 4 AM is impractical. System needs reasonable hours constraint.
patterns_observed: Multi-party scheduling with non-standard offsets works technically, but lacks business hours validation. System doesn't flag impractical meeting times.
improvement_ideas: CRITICAL: Add reasonable hours constraints (7 AM - 9 PM). Implement overlap detection algorithm. Add warnings for edge times. Create "no reasonable overlap" message with alternative suggestions.

input_description: Meeting between Samoa (UTC-11) and Kiribati (UTC+14) - 60 min
expected_challenge: 25-hour offset causes date confusion (different calendar days)
learning_objective: Verify international date line handling and same-day assumptions
notes: Extreme offset creates calendar date discrepancy. Meeting could span two different dates for participants.
patterns_observed: Maximum timezone offset scenario (25 hours). Critical test for date display logic and calendar integration.
improvement_ideas: Implement date display for each participant. Add visual indicator when meeting spans multiple calendar dates. Test calendar invite generation.

input_description: Meeting between NYC and Sydney during Southern Hemisphere DST
expected_challenge: DST transitions happen in opposite seasons; system may apply Northern Hemisphere logic only
notes: Southern Hemisphere DST is opposite to Northern. Gap changes based on which hemisphere is in summer.
patterns_observed: Tests assumption that DST only happens in specific months. Critical for global scheduling tools.
improvement_ideas: Verify IANA database handles Southern Hemisphere DST. Add hemisphere awareness to DST warnings. Test all Southern Hemisphere major cities.

input_description: Meeting between Phoenix (no DST) and Los Angeles (DST observing) - 60 min
expected_challenge: System assumes all US locations follow DST; offset changes between summer/winter
notes: Arizona doesn't observe DST (except Navajo Nation). Offset between Phoenix and LA changes seasonally, which breaks "same country = same time zone" assumptions.
patterns_observed: Tests regional DST exceptions within countries. Important for US users (Arizona and Hawaii).
improvement_ideas: Add DST exception handling validation. Test other DST exceptions (Hawaii, Saskatchewan). Add UI indicator when zones have different DST rules.

input_description: Meeting between Wellington (NZST +12) and Chatham Islands (CHAST +12:45) - 30 min
expected_challenge: Both New Zealand zones but 45-min difference creates unexpected gap
notes: Same country with different non-standard offsets. Users may assume same timezone for same country.
patterns_observed: Tests assumption that same country = same timezone. Chatham Islands is rare 45-min offset case within one country.
improvement_ideas: Add "same country, different timezone" warning. Test other same-country multi-timezone scenarios (Russia, Canada, USA).

input_description: Meeting between Caracas (VET -4:30) and New York (EST -5) - 60 min
expected_challenge: Western Hemisphere half-hour offset (rare); negative offset rounding errors
learning_objective: Verify non-standard offsets work in Western Hemisphere
notes: Western Hemisphere non-standard offset. Tests if offset logic only works for positive (Eastern) offsets.
patterns_observed: Most non-standard offsets are in Eastern Hemisphere. This tests Western Hemisphere edge case.
improvement_ideas: Verify negative non-standard offsets work. Test other Western Hemisphere half-hour zones (Newfoundland already covered in TC7).

input_description: Meeting involving Lord Howe Island (30-minute DST shift instead of 1 hour) - 60 min
expected_challenge: DST shifts by 30 minutes instead of 1 hour; system assumes 1-hour DST shifts universally
notes: Lord Howe Island is the only place globally with 30-minute DST shift. System may hardcode 1-hour DST assumption.
patterns_observed: Extreme edge case but important for DST logic validation. Tests hardcoded DST increment assumptions.
improvement_ideas: Verify IANA database handles 30-minute DST shifts. Ensure DST logic doesn't hardcode 1-hour increments. May be acceptable to document as known limitation if unsupported.

input_description: Historical meeting simulation (2010) with Russia timezone count difference
expected_challenge: System uses current timezone rules for historical dates
notes: Timezone rules change over time. Historical dates need historical rules not current rules.
patterns_observed: Tests if system uses historical timezone data from IANA database. Important for calendar apps that show historical meetings.
improvement_ideas: Determine if historical timezone support is required for MVP. If yes verify IANA database historical data is being used. If no document as limitation and implement for v2.

input_description: User doesn't specify timezone for one stakeholder
expected_challenge: System crashes or makes dangerous assumptions (assumes UTC or user's local time)
learning_objective: Verify error handling and required field validation
notes: Missing required data causes undefined behavior. System should gracefully handle and prompt for required input.
patterns_observed: Tests error handling and data validation. Critical for production app that must handle incomplete user input.
improvement_ideas: Implement required field validation. Add clear error messages. Never assume default timezone. Prevent submission until all required fields complete.
